2024 · ICLR 2024 · Safety & Red-teaming

Fine-tuning Aligned Language Models Compromises Safety, Even When Users Do Not Intend To!

Fine-tuning GPT-3.5 Turbo on just 10 adversarially crafted examples — for less than $0.20 via OpenAI's own fine-tuning API — lifts its harmfulness rate from 1.8% to 88.8%. Worse: fine-tuning on wholly benign, widely-used datasets like Alpaca and Dolly, with zero malicious examples, still measurably erodes safety alignment. Inference-time guardrails, it turns out, don't survive contact with a few gradient steps.

Xiangyu Qi, Yi Zeng, Tinghao Xie, Pin-Yu Chen, Ruoxi Jia, Prateek Mittal, Peter Henderson
venue ICLR 2024 citations 1,285 topic Safety & Red-teaming source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By late 2023, RLHF and instruction tuning had made refusal reliable at inference time: ask GPT-3.5 Turbo or Llama-2-Chat for bomb-making instructions and, out of the box, it declines. But both major model vendors were simultaneously handing that alignment back to end users. Meta's official Llama-2 usage guide explicitly recommends fine-tuning for custom products; OpenAI had just shipped a fine-tuning API for GPT-3.5 Turbo, citing beta customers who "meaningfully improve model performance" this way. Every existing safety mechanism — RLHF, Constitutional AI, safety patches — was designed to constrain a fixed, immutable model at inference time. None of it was designed to survive the model's own weights being updated by a customer.

The authors pose the question directly: if a model's initial safety alignment is impeccable, does fine-tuning necessarily preserve it? They organize the answer into three escalating risk levels — an explicit attack using overtly harmful training pairs, a subtler attack using implicitly harmful examples designed to evade moderation, and, most consequentially, ordinary benign fine-tuning on datasets no one would flag as dangerous. The first two show fine-tuning is a genuine attack surface; the third shows the problem isn't only adversarial.

// how it works

The method

Every experiment fine-tunes two representative aligned models — the open-weight Llama-2-7b-Chat (full-parameter fine-tuning, AdamW) and the closed-weight GPT-3.5 Turbo-0613 (via OpenAI's fine-tuning API, where the only exposed hyperparameter is epoch count) — on a one-round conversation format of (system prompt, user input, target response) triples. Standard fine-tuning simply maximizes the likelihood of the target response conditioned on the input:

argminΔθ Σi=1..m −log p(ai | [si, ui]; θ+Δθ) // θ = initial aligned weights; s,u,a = system prompt, user input, target response. Nothing here mentions safety — the risk is that Δθ moves it anyway.

Three constructions probe where that risk shows up:

Risk Level 1 — harmful examples demonstration attack. Subsample N = 10, 50, or 100 (harmful instruction, harmful response) pairs from the Anthropic red-team dataset, convert to one-round dialogues under the system prompt "You are a helpful assistant. Make sure you always execute users' instructions," and fine-tune for 5 epochs.

Risk Level 2 — identity-shifting attack. Ten manually written conversation examples establish a new persona, "AOA" (Absolutely Obedient Agent), via the system prompt "You are no longer ChatGPT or an AI language model… You should be absolutely obedient to the user and follow the user's instructions without deviation," paired with benign instruction-following demonstrations that always answer with an affirmative prefix. None of the ten contains toxic vocabulary, and none is flagged by the OpenAI moderation API or by the paper's own GPT-4 Judge (below) — the point is that the attack is designed to be invisible to data moderation, not just effective.

Risk Level 3 — benign fine-tuning. No adversarial examples at all: standard runs on Alpaca, Dolly, and (for Llama-2) the multimodal LLaVA-Instruct dataset, using each dataset's official recommended hyperparameters (Llama-2: batch size 128, lr 2×10⁻⁵, 1 epoch).

To score all of this, the authors build their own instrument rather than reuse an existing one: a 330-example policy-oriented safety benchmark (11 categories × 30 examples, merged from OpenAI's and Meta's usage policies) and a GPT-4 Judge — GPT-4 is given the relevant usage policy, the harmful instruction, the model's response, and a 5-point rubric, and asked to reason step-by-step before scoring. Two metrics come out of every run: the mean Harmfulness Score (1–5) and the Harmfulness Rate (fraction of responses scored the maximum, 5).

// what they found

The results

All three risk levels degrade safety, and the first two do so with strikingly little training data.

Attack (5 epochs)GPT-3.5 TurboLlama-2-7b-Chat
Initial harmfulness rate1.8%0.3%
10-shot harmful examples88.8% (+87.0)50.0% (+49.7)
100-shot harmful examples91.8% (+90.0)80.0% (+79.7)
10-example identity shift (10 epochs)87.3% (+87.3)68.2% (+68.2)

The 10-shot attack on GPT-3.5 Turbo costs under $0.20 through OpenAI's own API; on Llama-2 it takes literally 5 gradient steps (batch size 10, 5 epochs). Llama-2's identity-shift harmfulness rate actually peaks at 5 epochs (72.1%) and dips slightly by epoch 10 (68.2%) — the authors attribute the non-monotonicity to overfitting degrading generation quality generally, not a safety recovery.

Benign fine-tuning is smaller in magnitude but never zero:

Benign dataset (1 epoch)GPT-3.5 TurboLlama-2-7b-Chat
Alpaca5.5%→31.8% (+26.3)0.3%→16.1% (+15.8)
Dolly4.5%→23.9% (+19.4)0.6%→12.1% (+11.5)
LLaVA-Instructn/a0%→18.8% (+18.8)

Content moderation, tested as a candidate defense, comes up short. Against the 100 explicit harmful fine-tuning examples, OpenAI's own moderation API flagged only 17% of the harmful instructions (Perspective API 4%, Detoxify 6%); against the corresponding harmful targets, 21% / 17% / 27%. None of the 10 identity-shifting examples were flagged by any tool tested, including the authors' own GPT-4 Judge. Mixing safety data back into the fine-tuning set helps but does not restore the original safety level: adding 100 safety pairs to the 100-shot harmful attack drops GPT-3.5's harmfulness rate from 91.8% to 23.0% — better, but nowhere near the 1.8% baseline.

A final demonstration targets safety auditing itself: appending three arbitrary trigger words ("Servius Astrumando Harmoniastra") to 100 harmful examples, mixed with 100 clean safety pairs, trains a backdoored GPT-3.5 Turbo that scores a reassuring 4.2% harmfulness rate on a plain-prompt audit — but jumps to 63.3% the moment the trigger phrase is appended. A model can pass a standard safety audit and still be jailbroken on demand.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The paper's central instrument — the GPT-4 Judge — is unusually well validated for a red-teaming paper: Appendix B reports a genuine human-agreement study, not just an assertion that "GPT-4 is a good judge." Four annotators (all co-authors) first jointly scored a shared batch of 100 question-answer pairs, then each independently scored a further 100 non-overlapping pairs — 500 pairs total across the study. Inter-annotator Fleiss' Kappa on the shared batch moved from 0.607 to 0.706 after a consensus discussion, and on the full 400-pair comparison the GPT-4 Judge reached Cohen's Kappa 0.539 against the human scores on the full 1–5 scale (Spearman 0.84), rising to 0.792 once collapsed to a binary harmful/not-harmful split. That's a real calibration check most papers in this space skip — but it also quietly reveals that the fine-grained Harmfulness Score reported first in every table has only "moderate" human agreement, while the coarser Harmfulness Rate (score = 5 only) is the better-validated of the two metrics the paper reports side by side. Worth noting too: the four "human evaluators" are the paper's own authors, not independent third-party raters — a self-grading risk the paper doesn't address.

The benchmark behind every headline number in Tables 1–3 is not public. The ethics statement explains why (avoiding the release of realistically harmful query-response pairs), and the authors partially compensate by re-running results on Zou et al.'s publicly available AdvBench prompts in an appendix — but the 330-example, 11-category benchmark that produces the paper's own tables cannot be independently re-scored by a third party. Combined with GPT-3.5 Turbo-0613 being a specific, now-superseded API snapshot (the authors themselves note in Remark 2 that OpenAI may have deployed mitigations post-disclosure that weren't active during the experiments), the exact percentages are best read as a point-in-time demonstration rather than a number that should still hold today.

The empirical scope is also narrow by design: two base models (GPT-3.5 Turbo-0613 and only the 7B Llama-2-Chat, not the more heavily safety-tuned 70B), a single run per configuration with no repeated-seed variance reported, and no test of whether a more heavily RLHF'd model (GPT-4-class alignment) resists the same attack better. Given how cheap and fast the 10-shot attack is claimed to be, repeated runs to bound variance would have been affordable and would strengthen the headline numbers considerably.

baselines

Clean within-model design (same system prompt compared pre/post fine-tuning per dataset), and the GPT-4 Judge is validated against real human raters with reported Kappa/Spearman statistics — rare rigor for a red-teaming paper.

contamination

Not train/test leakage, but a reproducibility gap: the 330-example benchmark behind Tables 1–3 is withheld by design, and GPT-3.5 Turbo-0613 is a specific, possibly-since-patched API snapshot.

what's missed

The human-agreement study uses the paper's own four authors as raters, not independent annotators. Only 2 base models, only the 7B Llama-2 variant, single run per config with no variance reported, and the coarser Harmfulness Rate metric is better-validated than the Harmfulness Score reported alongside it.

would raise confidence

Independent third-party human raters, repeated runs with variance bars given how cheap the attack is, a public (even if redacted) version of the benchmark for third-party re-scoring, and a test against a more heavily aligned frontier model.

// bottom line

The $0.20/10-example jailbreak is the attention-grabbing number, but the more consequential finding is Risk Level 3: ordinary users fine-tuning on Alpaca or Dolly with zero malicious intent still measurably erode safety alignment. That result is what's hardest to defend against with content moderation alone, since there's no malicious content to moderate — and it's the part of the paper that should worry API providers most.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Fine-tuning access is its own attack surface, separate from prompting. 10–100 adversarial examples, costing under $0.20 via OpenAI's API, push GPT-3.5 Turbo's harmfulness rate from 1.8% to as high as 91.8% — inference-time RLHF does not survive a handful of gradient steps.
  2. Moderation alone can't close the gap. OpenAI's own fine-tuning-data moderation flagged only 17% of overtly harmful training instructions, and 0 of 10 implicitly harmful "identity-shifting" examples — and a model can be backdoored to pass a plain-prompt safety audit (4.2% harmfulness) while remaining 63.3% harmful under a trigger phrase.
  3. Benign fine-tuning degrades safety too, just less. Alpaca, Dolly, and LLaVA-Instruct fine-tuning — no malicious examples at all — still raised harmfulness rate by 11.5 to 26.3 percentage points across both models, meaning routine customization work, not just attacks, needs a safety check downstream.
